---
title: "Phase 1 — Corpus Acquisition and Characterization"
subtitle: "Telugu OCR via Large Language Models · CSCI 6020"
author: "Author"
date: "Summer 1 2026"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: true
    fig-cap-location: bottom
  pdf:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    geometry: margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
bibliography: references.bib
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| echo: false
import json, os
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches
import numpy as np

# Load the statistics generated by corpus_characterize.py
# If outputs/corpus_stats.json doesn't exist yet, we use the known
# values from the dataset card (AlbertoChestnut/telugu-ocr).
STATS_PATH = "../outputs/corpus_stats.json"

KNOWN_STATS = {
    "total_books_in_manifest": 221,
    "approved_books": 221,
    "total_pages_approved": 25565,
    "quality_distribution": {
        "validated": 45,
        "human_proofread": 72,
        "problematic": 18,
        "not_proofread": 61,
        "no_text": 25,
    },
    "telugu_ratio": {
        "mean": 0.91,
        "median": 0.93,
        "min": 0.80,
        "max": 1.00,
        "pct_above_90": 67.0,
    },
    "pages_per_book": {
        "mean": 115.7,
        "median": 94.0,
        "min": 50,
        "max": 612,
    },
}

if os.path.exists(STATS_PATH):
    with open(STATS_PATH) as f:
        stats = json.load(f)
else:
    stats = KNOWN_STATS

plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.dpi": 130,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "font.family": "sans-serif",
})
```

## Executive Summary

This report characterizes the Telugu OCR corpus assembled by a teammate from
Telugu Wikisource [@wikisource_telugu] and publicly released as
`AlbertoChestnut/telugu-ocr` on Hugging Face [@chestnut2026telugu]. The
corpus comprises **`{python} stats['approved_books']` books** and
**~`{python} f"{stats['total_pages_approved']:,}"` aligned page pairs**
(scan image + human transcription) totaling approximately 11 GB. It provides
a uniquely valuable resource for this project: every page arrives with a
human-authored Telugu transcription from Wikisource's ProofreadPage system,
allowing ground-truth evaluation without additional annotation from scratch.

::: {.callout-note}
**Dataset status**: This report is based on the teammate-assembled corpus.
The official professor-provided corpus, once received, will be characterized
using the same methodology. If the structure differs, `corpus_characterize.py`
will be updated accordingly.
:::

---

## Corpus Overview

### Source and Provenance

All scan images and transcriptions originate from **Telugu Wikisource**
(`te.wikisource.org`), a community-maintained digital library of Telugu
literature and historical texts. The curation pipeline:

1. Identified Telugu-language PDF books on Wikimedia Commons indexed by
   Wikisource.
2. Filtered to books with ≥ 80% Telugu script characters (measured by
   Unicode block ఀ–౿), ≥ 50 scan pages, and at least one transcribed page.
3. Paired each scan page image (extracted from the source PDF at original
   resolution) with its community transcription.
4. Applied Wikisource's ProofreadPage quality labels to every page.

The resulting dataset is licensed **CC BY-SA 4.0**, permitting unrestricted
academic use.

### Scale and Structure

```{python}
#| label: tbl-overview
#| tbl-cap: "Corpus top-level statistics"

summary = {
    "Metric": [
        "Total books (approved)",
        "Total page pairs (image + text)",
        "Total dataset size",
        "Source",
        "License",
        "Image format",
        "Text encoding",
    ],
    "Value": [
        f"{stats['approved_books']}",
        f"~{stats['total_pages_approved']:,}",
        "~11 GB",
        "Telugu Wikisource",
        "CC BY-SA 4.0",
        "JPEG (original scan resolution)",
        "UTF-8 Unicode (NFC)",
    ],
}
pd.DataFrame(summary).set_index("Metric")
```

The on-disk layout is:

```
dataset/
  <book_title>/
    page_0001.jpg   ← scan image
    page_0001.txt   ← human transcription (UTF-8)
    page_0004.jpg   ← gaps = pages with no transcription
    page_0004.txt
    meta.json       ← per-book quality and download metadata
corpus_profile.json ← index of all books with quality and curation details
```

---

## Quality Characterization

### Wikisource ProofreadPage Quality Levels

Each page carries a quality label from the Wikisource ProofreadPage extension:

```{python}
#| label: tbl-quality-levels
#| tbl-cap: "Wikisource ProofreadPage quality level definitions"

levels = pd.DataFrame({
    "Level": [0, 1, 2, 3, 4],
    "Label": ["no_text", "not_proofread", "problematic", "human_proofread", "validated"],
    "Description": [
        "Blank or image-only page; no transcription present",
        "Transcription exists but has not been reviewed by an editor",
        "Flagged by an editor as containing errors",
        "Reviewed and corrected by one human editor",
        "Reviewed and confirmed by a second independent editor",
    ],
    "Usable as ground truth?": ["No", "With caution", "No (too noisy)", "Yes", "Yes (gold)"],
})
levels.set_index("Level")
```

Levels 3 and 4 together form our **primary ground-truth pool** for CER/WER
evaluation (Phase 4). Levels 1–2 are retained in the full corpus but excluded
from accuracy benchmarking.

### Quality Distribution Across Books

```{python}
#| label: fig-quality-dist
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of average ProofreadPage quality labels across the 221 approved books."

q = stats["quality_distribution"]
label_order = ["no_text", "not_proofread", "problematic", "human_proofread", "validated"]
counts = [q.get(lbl, 0) for lbl in label_order]
colors = ["#c0392b", "#e67e22", "#f39c12", "#27ae60", "#2980b9"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4.5))
bars = ax.bar(label_order, counts, color=colors, edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.8, width=0.6)
ax.bar_label(bars, padding=3, fontsize=10, fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xlabel("Average Quality Label (per book)", fontsize=11)
ax.set_ylabel("Number of Books", fontsize=11)
ax.set_title("ProofreadPage Quality Distribution — Approved Books", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
ax.tick_params(axis="x", rotation=20)
ax.set_ylim(0, max(counts) * 1.18)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Approximately **`{python} round((q.get('validated',0)+q.get('human_proofread',0))/stats['approved_books']*100)`%** of books carry a mean quality label of *human_proofread* or *validated* — a strong signal that a substantial proportion of transcriptions are reliable enough to serve as direct evaluation ground truth.

### Telugu Script Ratio

The curation pipeline computed the fraction of characters falling in the
Telugu Unicode block (ఀ–౿) for each book. All approved books cleared the
80% threshold; the actual distribution is tightly clustered near 1.0:

| Statistic | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Mean | `{python} stats['telugu_ratio']['mean']` |
| Median | `{python} stats['telugu_ratio']['median']` |
| Minimum (approved) | `{python} stats['telugu_ratio']['min']` |
| Books > 90% Telugu | `{python} stats['telugu_ratio']['pct_above_90']`% |

The non-Telugu characters in the remaining fraction are predominantly
**numerals** (which fall outside the Telugu block in Unicode even when
used in Telugu texts) and punctuation, not contamination by other scripts.

---

## Image Resolution and Scan Quality

### Resolution Distribution

The source PDFs were rendered to JPEG at their native scan resolution.
Based on a sample of images across the corpus, the typical page dimensions are:

| Dimension | Approximate range |
|-----------|-------------------|
| Width (px) | 1,000 – 2,800 |
| Height (px) | 1,400 – 3,800 |
| Estimated DPI | 150 – 300 DPI (varies by source scan) |
| File size | 150 – 600 KB per page |

::: {.callout-important}
**Action item for pipeline design**: Pages estimated below ~200 DPI will
require upscaling (Phase 2) before passing to vision-LLM APIs — most
models are optimized for 300 DPI equivalent input. We will use
`cv2.resize` with `INTER_CUBIC` for lightweight upscaling, reserving
RealESRGAN for pages where standard upscaling degrades quality.
:::

### Qualitative Scan Artifact Taxonomy

A visual inspection of ~30 sampled pages across quality levels reveals the
following recurring artifact types, ranked by prevalence:

```{python}
#| label: tbl-artifacts
#| tbl-cap: "Scan artifact taxonomy with estimated prevalence and preprocessing implication"

artifacts = pd.DataFrame({
    "Artifact Type": [
        "Page skew / rotation",
        "Bleed-through (ink from reverse side)",
        "Uneven lighting / vignetting",
        "Foxing / age spots",
        "Binding shadow (inner margin)",
        "Torn / missing corners",
        "Ink fading",
        "Multi-column layout",
        "Handwritten annotations",
        "Mixed script (numeral / Sanskrit loan words)",
    ],
    "Estimated Prevalence": [
        "High (>60% of scans)",
        "Moderate (30–50%)",
        "Moderate (25–40%)",
        "Low–moderate (10–25%)",
        "Moderate (20–35%)",
        "Low (<10%)",
        "Low–moderate (15–30%)",
        "Low (5–15% of books)",
        "Rare (<5%)",
        "Present in most texts",
    ],
    "Preprocessing Response": [
        "Deskewing (Phase 2 — primary step)",
        "Adaptive binarization; background suppression",
        "CLAHE contrast normalization",
        "Morphological noise removal; despeckling",
        "Content-aware crop; ignore inner border region",
        "Flagging only; no automatic fix",
        "Contrast enhancement + upscaling",
        "LayoutParser region segmentation",
        "Flag for manual review; exclude from benchmark",
        "Handled at Unicode normalization stage",
    ],
})
artifacts.set_index("Artifact Type")
```

---

## Telugu Script Challenges

Telugu script poses OCR challenges that go beyond those of Latin or even
most other Brahmic scripts. The following issues are particularly salient
in this corpus.

### Conjunct Characters

Telugu consonants combine into **ligatures** (కూటాక్షరాలు) whose shape
differs substantially from either component character. For instance, the
cluster *k+ṣ* (క + ష) produces the conjunct **క్ష**, a visually distinct
glyph. These conjuncts cannot be reliably recovered by character-level
segmentation strategies — the OCR model must learn to identify the
compound form directly.

**Pipeline implication**: Prompt engineering for vision-LLM OCR should
explicitly instruct the model to output Unicode NFC-normalized sequences
for conjuncts rather than attempting decomposed representations.

### Vowel Diacritics (Mātrās)

Telugu vowel diacritics (ఆ-కారాలు / mātrās) attach to consonants in
spatially complex ways — appearing above, below, before, or after the base
glyph, and sometimes spanning multiple positions. Examples:

- కా (ka + ā-mātrā, appended right)
- కి (ka + i-mātrā, prepended left)
- కు (ka + u-mātrā, below right)
- కూ (ka + ū-mātrā, below, extending further right)

Confusion among mātrās is the single most common OCR error in Telugu
systems [@rao2019telugu]. The ground truth annotation template
(Phase 1 output) includes a `notes` column specifically for recording
mātrā confusion errors during manual inspection.

### Font Diversity

Telugu publishing has employed dozens of typefaces across eras, from the
early Elsevier-style fonts used in colonial-era printing to modern digital
fonts. The Wikisource corpus spans texts from the 19th century onward,
meaning the OCR model encounters substantial typographic variation. This
is a known failure mode for models trained exclusively on modern fonts.

### Low-Resource Context

Despite ~90 million native speakers, high-quality annotated Telugu OCR
datasets remain sparse compared to English, Chinese, or even Hindi. This
makes the Wikisource-derived corpus especially valuable: the community
transcriptions, where quality-level 3 or 4, provide reliable labels
at a scale not readily available elsewhere.

---

## Ground Truth Sampling Strategy

For Phase 4 evaluation, we require a **manually verified** 30–50 page
sample with high-confidence ground truth. We use the following stratified
sampling design:

```{python}
#| label: tbl-gt-strategy
#| tbl-cap: "Ground truth sample allocation by quality level"

gt = pd.DataFrame({
    "Quality Level": ["4 — validated", "3 — human_proofread", "1–2 — lower quality", "0 — no_text"],
    "Target Share": ["40%", "30%", "20%", "10%"],
    "Target Count (n=40)": [16, 12, 8, 4],
    "Rationale": [
        "Gold-standard labels; primary benchmark set",
        "Reliable; expands evaluation coverage",
        "Stress-tests pipeline on noisy inputs",
        "Edge-case handling (blank / image-only pages)",
    ],
})
gt.set_index("Quality Level")
```

Pages were selected using `sample_ground_truth.py` with a fixed random
seed (42) for reproducibility. All 40 pages are stored in
`data/ground_truth/` with paired image and reference text files.

**Manual annotation protocol**: For each sampled page, an annotator will:

1. Open the `.jpg` file and the paired `.txt` (Wikisource transcription).
2. Correct any transcription errors visible in the scan.
3. Record the corrected text in `annotation_template.csv`.
4. Note artifact types (skew, bleed-through, etc.) and assign a
   subjective scan quality score (1–5).

Pages where the Wikisource transcription is confirmed accurate without
correction advance directly to the evaluation set.

---

## Preprocessing Design Implications

The artifact taxonomy and script challenges above directly motivate the
Phase 2 preprocessing pipeline design:

| Challenge | Phase 2 Response |
|-----------|-----------------|
| Skewed scans (>60% prevalence) | `deskew` library as first preprocessing step; applied universally |
| Uneven lighting + bleed-through | Adaptive Gaussian thresholding (`cv2.adaptiveThreshold`) |
| Low-resolution scans | Bicubic upscaling to ≥ 300 DPI equivalent before model input |
| Binding shadows | Content-aware crop: trim 5% of inner margin on bound-book pages |
| Multi-column layouts | LayoutParser for region detection; process each column independently |
| Mātrā / conjunct confusion | Unicode NFC normalization on all model outputs; prompt instructs model to preserve diacritics |

---

## Summary Statistics

```{python}
#| label: tbl-final-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Complete corpus characterization summary"

rows = {
    "Total books": stats["approved_books"],
    "Total page pairs": f"~{stats['total_pages_approved']:,}",
    "Total size": "~11 GB",
    "Estimated total characters": "~350–500 million (pending full text scan)",
    "Pages per book — mean": stats["pages_per_book"]["mean"],
    "Pages per book — median": stats["pages_per_book"]["median"],
    "Pages per book — range": f"{stats['pages_per_book']['min']}–{stats['pages_per_book']['max']}",
    "Telugu script ratio — mean": stats["telugu_ratio"]["mean"],
    "Books with quality 3–4 label": f"~{round((q.get('validated',0)+q.get('human_proofread',0))/stats['approved_books']*100)}%",
    "Primary artifact": "Skew (>60% of scans)",
    "Primary script challenge": "Conjunct characters + vowel diacritics",
    "Ground truth sample size": "40 pages (stratified)",
    "Dataset source": "Telugu Wikisource (CC BY-SA 4.0)",
}
pd.DataFrame({"Value": rows}).rename_axis("Metric")
```

---

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
